title: Get Rich Quick
introduction: Jordan gets this DM out of nowhere. Tap anything suspicious, then press Submit.

script.text: Hey! Saw your profile and thought you'd be a good fit for something I've been doing. Made €2000 last week through a crypto trading system my mentor taught me — takes about 20 mins a day. I only share it with a few people. You just need €100 to start and I'll show you exactly what to do. Interested?
script.explanation: Cold DMs offering investment opportunities are almost universally scams. '€2000 in a week' from a stranger who 'happened to see your profile' — no legitimate investment works this way. The 'only a few people' framing creates false exclusivity.

script.text: My mentor has a special algorithm — I can't explain the full details but it predicts market movements. You send me the €100 via Revolut, I invest it through the platform, and within 48 hours I send you back €300. Once you see it working you can invest more.
script.flag_label: Secret system + send money to individual
script.explanation: No legitimate investment system is secret, and no legitimate investment asks you to send money to a private individual via Revolut. Revolut transfers to individuals are extremely difficult to recover. The promised 3x return in 48 hours is mathematically absurd.

script.text: Can you show me proof it works?

Sure! [Sends screenshots of fake bank transfers showing large balances]

These are from my clients this week. But listen — I only have 2 spots left and someone else is about to take the last one. You need to decide today.
script.explanation: Screenshots of bank balances are trivially easy to fake with free tools. 'Only 2 spots left' and 'decide today' are scarcity and urgency tactics to prevent research. Any investment that pressures you to decide before you can think is a scam.

script.text: I'm going to look you up first and do some research.
Honestly if you wait you'll miss out — the people who overthink always regret it. This is a limited window. Your call.
script.explanation: Any offer that discourages you from doing research is a scam. 'Overthinking' is what scammers call checking facts. Legitimate investments welcome scrutiny. Jordan's instinct to research was exactly right.

learning_objectives: Unsolicited investment DMs are almost always scams
No legitimate investment guarantees returns — it's illegal to promise
Never send money to individuals via Revolut for investments
Screenshots of earnings are easily faked
Any pressure to decide quickly is a manipulation tactic — slow down
debrief:
This is a classic investment scam. The script barely varies: cold contact, impressive claims, fake proof, urgency, money sent via untraceable method.

**The first proof payment sometimes arrives** — funded by earlier victims — to build trust before a larger amount is requested. Then everything stops.

**Jordan did the right thing** by asking for proof and saying they'd research first. Research is the correct response to any unexpected investment offer. The moment someone discourages that, you have your answer.
